title: Step‑by‑Step: Herb‑Infused Olive Oil Using Five‑Blade Shears
keyword: herb infused olive oil five blade shears
meta_description: Learn how to make professional herb infused olive oil five blade shears style. Save time on chopping and get a perfectly consistent mince every time.
html:
There is nothing quite like a bottle of homemade herb-infused oil sitting on your counter. Whether you're drizzling it over a fresh caprese salad or using it as a base for a gourmet marinade, the flavor profile is leagues beyond anything you'll find in a mass-produced plastic bottle. The secret to a great infusion isn't just the quality of the oil—it's the surface area of the herbs.

Traditionally, this means spending twenty minutes with a chef's knife, bruising your basil and creating a mountain of cutting board scraps. That is where the right tool changes the game. To get a consistent, fine cut that releases maximum aromatic oils, I use herb infused olive oil five blade shears. By cutting five stems at once, you achieve a uniform mince in seconds, ensuring the flavors distribute evenly throughout your oil.

Choosing Your Herbs and Oil

Before you reach for the scissors, you need the right ingredients. For a classic Mediterranean blend, I recommend a mix of fresh rosemary, thyme, and oregano. If you want something brighter and more contemporary, go with basil, chives, and parsley. The key is to use fresh, organic herbs whenever possible, as the volatile oils are what provide that punchy flavor.

As for the oil, stick to a high-quality extra virgin olive oil (EVOO). Since this is an infusion, the oil acts as the carrier for the herbs' flavors. A refined oil won't have the same peppery notes or richness, which can leave your final product tasting flat. Make sure your herbs are completely dry after washing; any lingering water can introduce bacteria or cause the oil to separate.

The Technique: Using Herb Scissors for the Perfect Mince

When making herb infused olive oil five blade shears style, the goal is to avoid bruising the leaves. When you chop with a knife, you often crush the cells of the herb, which can lead to oxidation (that unappealing brown color). The Herb Scissors act differently; they slice cleanly through the stems and leaves in one motion.

Gather a small bunch of your chosen herbs and hold them firmly. Snip directly into a glass bowl or a tasting jar. Because these shears mince cilantro, chives, and basil in one snip, you can move through a large amount of greenery in about a quarter of the time it takes to use a knife. Once you're finished, use the included cleaning comb to clear out any stuck bits of greenery—this keeps the blades sharp and prevents cross-contamination between different herb batches.

Step-by-Step Infusion Process

Now that your herbs are finely minced, it's time to combine them with your oil. You have two choices: a cold infusion or a warm infusion. Cold infusion takes longer but preserves the freshest, "grassy" notes of the herbs. Warm infusion is faster and creates a deeper, more integrated flavor.

  1. Prep the Vessel: Sterilize a glass jar or bottle with boiling water and dry it completely.
  2. Add the Mince: Pour your freshly snipped herbs into the bottom of the jar.
  3. Pour: Fill the jar with extra virgin olive oil, ensuring the herbs are completely submerged.
  4. Seal and Shake: Close the lid tightly and give it a vigorous shake to distribute the mince.
  5. Steep: For cold infusion, store in a cool, dark place for 1-2 weeks. For warm infusion, place the jar in a pot of simmering water (bain-marie) for 20 minutes, then let it cool.
  6. Strain (Optional): If you prefer a clear oil, strain it through a cheesecloth; otherwise, leave the minced herbs in for a rustic look.

Storing Your Herb Infused Olive Oil

Safety is paramount when dealing with garlic or fresh herbs in oil. Fresh herbs contain moisture, which can potentially lead to botulism if stored incorrectly at room temperature for too long. To keep your oil safe and flavorful, always store your finished infusion in the refrigerator.

When refrigerated, the oil will stay fresh for about two weeks. If you notice the oil becoming cloudy or solidifying, simply let it sit at room temperature for an hour before using. To extend the shelf life, some home cooks add a pinch of salt or a squeeze of lemon juice, though the fridge is your best bet for safety.

Cleaning Up Your Gear

The biggest complaint people have with multi-blade tools is the cleanup. However, the Herb Scissors are designed with this in mind. After you've finished your prep, don't just rinse them under the tap. Use the cleaning comb to scrape the residue from between the blades first.

Once the bulk of the herbs are gone, a quick wash with warm soapy water and a thorough dry will prevent any rust or buildup. Keeping your shears clean ensures that your next batch of infused oil is just as crisp and clean as the first.

FAQ

Can I use dried herbs instead of fresh?

You can, but the result is different. Dried herbs don't have the same aromatic intensity as fresh. If using dried, you can skip the five-blade shears and simply stir the herbs into warm oil. For the best flavor, always go fresh.

Why did my infused oil turn brown?

This is usually caused by oxidation. This happens if the herbs were bruised during chopping or if the oil was exposed to too much light and air. Using a clean slice from the Herb Scissors and storing the oil in a dark bottle helps prevent this.

How many herbs can I mix in one batch?

It depends on your palate, but I recommend limiting it to three complementary herbs. For example, basil, parsley, and chive work well together. Adding too many different flavors can result in a "muddy" taste where no single herb stands out.
